claims: A localized region of right prefrontal cortex (PFC) is preferentially sensitive to individual item learning during the early stages of the experiment.
The left PFC region is sensitive to grammar learning which occurred across the entire course of the experiment.
The early increase in activation associated with item learning was attenuated as the experiment progressed.
There were no significant effects of item learning on the measured path strengths.
Rule learning was associated with a decrease in right fronto-parietal connectivity.
Rule learning was associated with an increase in connectivity between left and right PFC.
Left fronto-parietal connectivity path strength increased.
Right fronto-parietal connectivity path strength from right PFC to left parietal cortex decreased.
Both R and K judgments for studied words were associated with enhanced responses in left prefrontal and left parietal cortices relative to N judgments for unstudied words.
The opposite pattern was observed in bilateral temporoccipital regions and amygdalae.
R judgments for studied words were associated with enhanced responses in anterior left prefrontal, left parietal, and posterior cingulate regions relative to K judgments.
At study, a posterior left prefrontal region exhibited an enhanced response to words subsequently given R versus K judgments.
The response of this region during recognition did not differentiate R and K judgments.
K judgments for studied words were associated with enhanced responses in right lateral and medial prefrontal cortex relative to R judgments for studied words.
K judgments for studied words were associated with enhanced responses in right lateral and medial prefrontal cortex relative to N judgments for unstudied words.
The responses of different brain regions dissociate according to the phenomenology associated with memory retrieval.
For man-made objects, the only category-specific effect was in the left posterior middle temporal cortex.
The left posterior middle temporal cortex was most active for drawings of tools.
Drawings of animals and fruit enhanced activity in bilateral anterior temporal and right posterior middle temporal cortices relative to drawings of man-made objects.
These effects with natural objects were not observed when the stimuli were coloured appropriately to facilitate identification.
Activation in the same right hemisphere areas was observed for viewing and matching unfamiliar non-objects relative to naming and matching man-made objects.
In the right hemisphere, differences between processing natural relative to man-made objects overlap with the effects of increasing demands on object identification.
In the left hemisphere, the effects are more consistent with functional specialization within the semantic system.
The main effect of DMTS compared with DNMTS across both delay intervals was associated with significant activation in bilateral head of caudate.
The main effect of DMTS compared with DNMTS across both delay intervals was associated with significant activation in medial orbitofrontal cortex.
DNMTS compared with DMTS was associated with significant activation in mediodorsal thalamus.
DNMTS compared with DMTS was associated with significant activation in bilateral lateral orbitofrontal cortex.
DNMTS compared with DMTS was associated with significant activation in left premotor cortex.
The main effect of short compared with long delay across both tasks was associated with significantly greater activity in occipital cortices.
The main effect of short compared with long delay across both tasks was associated with significantly greater activity in parietal cortices.
Long compared with short delay was associated with significantly greater activity in temporal cortices.
Long compared with short delay was associated with significantly greater activity in ventrolateral frontal cortices.
DMTS and DNMTS are not equivalent.
The precise neural implementation of these tasks is a dynamic function of delay interval.
Both groups showed an interference effect.
The ADHD group failed to activate the ACcd during the Counting Stroop.
ACcd activity was significantly higher in the control group.
ADHD subjects did activate a frontostriatal-insular network.
ACcd hypoactivity was not caused by globally poor neuronal responsiveness.
Activation in a dorsal midlateral region of the right prefrontal cortex was associated with increased contextual monitoring demands.
A more ventral region of the right prefrontal cortex showed retrieval-related activation that was independent of task instructions.
Active movement was associated with activation of the contralateral primary sensorimotor cortex.
Active movement was associated with activation of the premotor cortex.
Active movement was associated with activation of the supplementary motor area (SMA).
Active movement was associated with activation of bilateral secondary somatosensory areas.
Active movement was associated with activation of basal ganglia.
Active movement was associated with activation of ipsilateral cerebellum.
The contralateral primary somatosensory areas were activated by the passive movement.
The contralateral secondary somatosensory areas were activated by the passive movement.
The increase in task difficulty was associated with increased regional cerebral blood flow in the posterior part of the right inferior temporal gyrus.
The increase in task difficulty was associated with increased regional cerebral blood flow in the anterior part of the right fusiform gyrus.
It took significantly longer to make object decisions on natural objects than on artefacts in the difficult object decision tasks.
Natural objects recruited larger parts of the right inferior temporal gyrus compared with artefacts as task difficulty increased.
Natural objects recruited larger parts of the anterior fusiform gyrus compared with artefacts as task difficulty increased.
Differential responses (CS+ vs CS-) related to conditioning were observed in the anterior cingulate and anterior insula.
Differential responses were observed in the amygdala and hippocampus.
A time x stimulus interaction indicates rapid adaptation of CS+-specific responses in the medial temporal lobe.
Additional activation of the anterior hippocampus was observed in the present experiment.
The anterior hippocampus maintains a memory trace between the offset of the CS+ and the delayed onset of the US to enable associative learning in trace conditioning.
For the arithmetic relative to the other tasks, results for all eight subjects revealed bilateral activation in Brodmann's area 44.
For the arithmetic relative to the other tasks, results for all eight subjects revealed bilateral activation in dorsolateral prefrontal cortex (areas 9 and 10).
For the arithmetic relative to the other tasks, results for all eight subjects revealed bilateral activation in inferior and superior parietal areas.
For the arithmetic relative to the other tasks, results for all eight subjects revealed bilateral activation in lingual and fusiform gyri.
Activation was stronger on the left for all subjects at Brodmann's area 44.
Activation was stronger on the left for all subjects in the parietal cortices.
No activation was observed in the arithmetic task in the angular gyrus.
No activation was observed in the arithmetic task in the supramarginal gyrus.
No activation was observed in the arithmetic task in the basal ganglia.
The angular gyrus was significantly deactivated by the verification task relative to both the magnitude judgment and control tasks for every subject.
The supramarginal gyrus was significantly deactivated by the verification task relative to both the magnitude judgment and control tasks for every subject.
The magnitude task relative to the control activated bilateral inferior parietal cortex in five subjects.

Successive subtraction of images obtained from five tasks revealed a network of left-lateralised areas, including posterior temporal regions, supramarginal gyri, Broca's area and dorsolateral premotor cortex.
Verbal item information is represented in left posterior temporal areas.
Short-term storage of phonological information occurs in left supramarginal gyrus.
Left dorsolateral premotor cortex is involved in the maintenance of temporal order.
Broca's area supports the articulatory processes required for phonological recoding of visual stimuli.
Wernicke's area displayed a negative correlation with temporal predictability.
The right homolog of Wernicke's area displayed a negative correlation with temporal predictability.
The negative correlation with temporal predictability in Wernicke's area and its right homolog is independent of individuals' conscious awareness of the sequence.
A widespread network of cortical regions displayed activity that correlated with the predictability when individuals were made aware of the underlying sequential predictability.
Conscious processing of predictability resulted in a positive correlation to activity in right prefrontal cortex.
Conscious processing of predictability resulted in a negative correlation in posterior parietal cortex.
Fields of activation observed as a main effect for all three modalities were located bilaterally in the SMA, CMAr, contralateral PMD, primary motor (M1), and primary somatosensory cortex (SI).
The activation in M1 engaged areas 4a and 4p and expanded into area 6.
The activation in SI engaged areas 3b, 1, and extended into somatosensory association areas and the supramarginal gyrus posteriorly.
Significant activations that were specific for each modality were found in the respective sensory association cortices.
No modality specific regions were found in the motor areas.
Fields in the anterior cingulate cortex, rostral to the CMAr, consistently showed significant negative correlation with mean reaction time (RT) in all three tasks.
Simple reaction time tasks activate many subdivisions of the motor cortices.
Information from different sensory modalities converge onto the common structures: the contralateral areas 4a, 4p, 3b, 1, the PMD, and bilaterally on the SMA and the CMAr.
The anterior cingulate cortex might be a key structure which determine the speed of reaction in simple RT tasks.
The transcription task yielded lateralized activation of the left posterior inferior temporal cortex.
The mental recall task yielded lateralized activation of the left posterior inferior temporal cortex.
Oral reading did not produce similar activation of the left posterior inferior temporal cortex.
Semantic judgement did not produce similar activation of the left posterior inferior temporal cortex.
The left PITC plays an important role in writing kanji through retrieval of their visual graphic images.
There is language-specific cerebral organization of writing.
The parametric increase of winning was associated with left amygdala activation.
The parametric increase of losing was associated with right amygdala activation.
The human amygdala differentially responds to changes in magnitude of positive or negative reinforcement conveyed by lexical stimuli.
Healthy volunteers and patients both exhibited bilateral activation (right higher than left) of the parahippocampal gyrus during the retrieval.
This effect was more marked in the control subjects.
In contrast to the control subjects, patients exhibited consistent and extensive left prefrontal activations in all the memory tasks.
Verbal memory tasks did not involve the same functional patterns in patients and healthy volunteers.

Contrasted against a silent baseline, listening to the tones induced widespread activation in the temporal cortex, including Heschl's gyrus (HG), planum temporale, superior temporal gyrus (STG), and superior temporal sulcus.
No additional auditory areas were recruited in the response to modulated tones compared to static tones.
There was an increase in the response in the STG, anterior to HG.
Relative to passive listening, the active task increased the response in the STG, posterior to HG.
The active task also recruited regions in the frontal and parietal cortex and subcortical areas.
Preferential responses to the changing spectro-temporal properties of the stimuli and to the target-discrimination task involve distinct, non-overlapping areas of the secondary auditory cortex.
Differences in sensory activation were not masked by the effects of attention.
SI activation increased with current amplitude.
SI did not show significant activation during stimulation at I(min).
SII activation did not depend on current amplitude.
The posterior parietal cortex appeared to be activated at I(min).
The I(min) response in SII significantly increased by selective attention compared to I(min) without attention.
At I(max) significant SI activity was observed only in the contralateral hemisphere.
At I(max) significant SI activity was observed in the ipsilateral cerebellum.
Distributed activation in the human somatosensory cortical system due to median nerve stimulation was observed using fMRI.
SI appears to be exclusively activated on the contralateral side of the stimulated hand at I(max).
Only SII remains significantly activated in case of lower current values.
Selective attention only enhances SII activity.
Subjects with autistic disorder differed significantly from controls in the activity of cerebellar, mesolimbic and temporal lobe cortical regions of the brain when processing facial expressions.
Subjects with autistic disorder did not activate a cortical 'face area' when explicitly appraising expressions.
Subjects with autistic disorder did not activate the left amygdala region when implicitly processing emotional facial expressions.
Subjects with autistic disorder did not activate the left cerebellum when implicitly processing emotional facial expressions.
High-functioning people with autistic disorder have biological differences from controls when consciously and unconsciously processing facial emotions.
These differences are most likely to be neurodevelopmental in origin.
Normal readers showed left prefrontal activity in response to rapidly changing, relative to slowly changing, nonlinguistic acoustic stimuli.
Dyslexic readers showed no differential left frontal response.
Two dyslexic readers showed increased activity in left prefrontal cortex after training.
Left prefrontal regions are plastic enough in adulthood to develop such differential sensitivity after intensive training.
Neural activity in the antero-dorsal prefrontal cortex increased only in attentional set shifting.
Switch costs were significant in attentional set shifting.
Activity in the postero-ventral prefrontal cortex increased in set shifting.
Activity in the postero-ventral prefrontal cortex increased in reversing stimulus-response associations.
Switch costs were absent in reversing stimulus-response associations.
Distinct regions in the human prefrontal cortex provide different levels of attention control in response selection.
The antero-dorsal prefrontal cortex may be critical for higher order control of attention, i.e. attentional set shifting.
The postero-ventral prefrontal cortex may be related to a lower level of shift, i.e. reorganizing stimulus-response associations.
Both early and late performance involve areas in the cerebellum, prefrontal, mid-temporal, extrastriate, and parietal cortices.
The particular regions within these broad areas differed for the two points of performance.
The strongest dissociation between early and late performance involved the corpus striatum, thalamus, and cingulate gyrus.
The neural circuit implementing this simple task varied over a relatively brief window of practice.
Interval and ordinal information led to activations within the same neural network.
Direct comparisons revealed significant differences in brain activations between processing interval and ordinal information.

The pre-supplementary motor area, lateral premotor cortex, frontal opercular cortex, basal ganglia, and left lateral cerebellar cortex were activated significantly more strongly by interval information.

The supplementary motor area, frontal eye field, primary motor cortex, primary somatosensory cortex, cuneus, medial cerebellar cortex, and thalamus were activated more strongly by ordinal information.

Serial encoding before reproduction led to higher activations than serial encoding before monitoring in the pre-supplementary motor area, supplementary motor area, primary motor cortex, and medial cerebellar cortex.
Sequential representation depends on both ordinal and interval aspects as well as motor requirements.
Predominantly left amygdala and substantia innominata activity was observed during the presentation of nonmasked fearful faces relative to fixation.
Activation in the left amygdala and substantia innominata decreased with repeated exposure to the faces.
Adults showed increased left amygdala activity for fearful faces relative to neutral faces.
Children showed greater amygdala activity with neutral faces than with fearful faces.
Boys but not girls showed less amygdala activity with repeated exposure to the fearful faces.
Specific activities in association with manipulating process were identified in the right middle (BA 9/46).
Specific activities in association with manipulating process were identified in the left precentral gyrus (BA 6).
Activated areas specific to maintaining process were detected in the right middle (right BA 11/10).
Activated areas specific to maintaining process were detected in the medial (BA 6) frontal gyri.
Activated areas specific to maintaining process were detected in the right inferior parietal lobule (BA 40).
Activated areas specific to maintaining process were detected in the left middle (BA 9).
Activated areas specific to maintaining process were detected in the left inferior frontal gyri (BA 44).
Process-nonspecific activated areas common to two processes were identified in the right inferior frontal gyrus (BA 47).
Process-nonspecific activated areas common to two processes were identified in the left superior parietal lobule (BA 7).
The two processes of verbal working memory were clearly discriminated.
The two essential processes of manipulation and maintenance in working memory activate process-specific areas.
The two essential processes of manipulation and maintenance in working memory activate overlapping (process-nonspecific) areas.
The patterns of combination were definitely different.
Unilateral stimuli produced normal responses in V1 and extrastriate areas.
In bilateral events, left faces that were not perceived activated right V1.
In bilateral events, left faces that were not perceived activated inferior temporal cortex.
In bilateral events, left faces that were not perceived evoked nonsignificantly reduced N1 potentials.
In bilateral events, left faces that were not perceived evoked preserved face-specific negative potentials at 170 ms.
When left faces were perceived, the same stimuli produced greater activity in a distributed network of areas including right V1 and cuneus, bilateral fusiform gyri, and left parietal cortex.
Effective connectivity between visual, parietal, and frontal areas increased during perception of faces.
Activity can occur in V1 and ventral temporal cortex without awareness.
Coupling with dorsal parietal and frontal areas may be critical for such activity to afford conscious perception.

Triple-step saccades strongly activated the regions of the frontal eye fields, the adjacent ventral premotor cortex, the supplementary eye fields, the anterior cingulate cortex and several posterior parietal foci in the superior parietal lobule, the precuneus, and the middle and posterior portion of the intraparietal sulcus.
The right intraparietal sulcus and parts of the frontal and supplementary eye fields are more involved in the execution of triple-step saccades than in the other saccade tasks.
The supplementary eye field essentially controls the triggering of memorized saccadic sequences.

Activation near the middle portion of the right intraparietal sulcus appears to reflect the necessary spatial computations, including the use of extraretinal information about a saccadic eye displacement for updating the spatial representation of the second or third target of the triple-step sequence.
The hippocampus was similarly activated for True and False items.
The parahippocampal gyrus was more activated for True than for False items.
Bilateral dorsolateral PFC was more activated for True and False items than for New items.
Left ventrolateral PFC was more activated for New than for True and False items.
Precuneus and lateral parietal regions were more activated for True and False than for New items.
Orbitofrontal cortex and cerebellar regions were more activated for False than for True items.
Activity in anterior MTL regions does not distinguish True from False.
Activity in posterior MTL regions distinguishes True from False.

Target processing by control participants was associated with bilateral activation in the anterior superior temporal gyri, inferior and superior parietal lobules, and activation in anterior and posterior cingulate, thalamus, and right lateral frontal cortex.

For the schizophrenic patients, selective deficits were observed in both the extent and strength of activation associated with target processing in the right lateral frontal cortex, thalamus, bilateral anterior superior temporal gyrus, anterior and posterior cingulate, and right inferior and superior parietal lobules.
Sexual dimorphisms of adult brain volumes were more evident in the cortex.
Women had larger volumes, relative to cerebrum size, particularly in frontal and medial paralimbic cortices.
Men had larger volumes, relative to cerebrum size, in frontomedial cortex.
Men had larger volumes, relative to cerebrum size, in the amygdala.
Men had larger volumes, relative to cerebrum size, in the hypothalamus.

Compared to other brain areas assessed in this study, there was greater sexual dimorphism among brain areas that are homologous with those identified in animal studies showing greater levels of sex steroid receptors during critical periods of brain development.

Correct identification of old words and correct rejection of new related words show common activation in the left middle frontal gyrus, the precuneus, the retrosplenial cortex, the left parahippocampal gyrus, and the thalamus.
Correct responses to old words additionally activate the frontomedian wall, the posterior cingulate cortex, and the nucleus accumbens.
Correct rejections of related new words are associated with additional activation of the right middle frontal gyrus.
False-positive responses to semantically related new words are associated with enhanced activation in the frontomedian wall.
Statistically significant spatial covariances were found for some, but not all comparisons.
Nine-parameter spatial normalization does not account for all the predictive value of structural or functional locations.
Nine-parameter spatial normalization may be improved upon by using selected sulcal and functional landmarks.
Recognition of words presented in emotionally negative relative to emotionally neutral contexts was associated with enhanced activity in right dorsolateral prefrontal cortex.
Recognition of words presented in emotionally negative relative to emotionally neutral contexts was associated with enhanced activity in left amygdala.
Recognition of words presented in emotionally negative relative to emotionally neutral contexts was associated with enhanced activity in hippocampus.
Recognition of words presented in emotionally negative relative to emotionally neutral contexts was associated with enhanced activity in right lingual gyrus.
Recognition of words presented in emotionally negative relative to emotionally neutral contexts was associated with enhanced activity in posterior cingulate cortex.
Recognition of words from positive relative to neutral contexts was associated with increased activity in bilateral prefrontal cortices.
Recognition of words from positive relative to neutral contexts was associated with increased activity in bilateral orbitofrontal cortices.
Recognition of words from positive relative to neutral contexts was associated with increased activity in left anterior temporal lobe.
Concurrently performed visual and somatosensory reaction time (RT) tasks engage almost identical volumes of cortical and subcortical motor structures.
Dual RT tasks engaged additional cortical regions that are not activated by the component RT tasks had they been performed as single tasks.
When the inter-stimulus interval was <300 ms, the first task interfered with the second.
A field in the right inferior frontal gyrus (RIFG) appeared with activity correlated with the increased RT to the second stimulus.
This activation was spatially distinct from the cortical activity of the main effect of dual task performance.
The performance of single RT tasks, dual RT tasks and dual RT tasks that interfere differ psychophysically.
The performance of single RT tasks, dual RT tasks and dual RT tasks that interfere differ in the brain structures subserving these tasks.
A short occupancy of the common motor structures can explain the interference effect.
The increased activity of the RIFG correlated with the interference effect is very likely to be a specific outcome of situations where two concurrent tasks interfere with each other.
The brain appears to recruit the RIFG for a subsequent (delayed) response when there is interference between dual tasks.
The voxel-wise analysis detected frontal (right > left) petalia.
The voxel-wise analysis detected occipital (left > right) petalia.
The analysis confirmed the presence of left-greater-than-right asymmetries in the planum temporale.
The analysis confirmed the presence of left-greater-than-right asymmetries in the angular gyrus.
No significant asymmetry was detected in the anterior language regions.
Previously described asymmetries in the cingulate sulcus were right-greater-than-left.
Previously described asymmetries in the caudate nucleus were right-greater-than-left.
Highly significant asymmetries that were not reported before were observed in the anterior insular cortex (right > left).
The above asymmetries were observed in men and women.
The supplementary motor area was significantly more active during the gentle force condition than during either of the other conditions in all subjects.
The cingulate motor area was significantly more active during the gentle force condition than during either of the other conditions in all subjects.
The supplementary and cingulate motor areas were more active during the gentle force condition despite weaker contractions of the hand muscles.
The left primary sensorimotor cortex was more strongly activated during gentle than during normal grasping.
The ventral premotor cortex was more strongly activated during gentle than during normal grasping.
The left posterior parietal cortex was more strongly activated during gentle than during normal grasping.
These regions are specifically involved in dexterous scaling of fingertip forces during object manipulation.
Brain activity specific to "biological motion" recognition arose in the lateral cerebellum.
Brain activity specific to "biological motion" recognition arose in a region in the lateral occipital cortex.
Additional areas significantly activated during the biological motion recognition task involved both dorsal and ventral extrastriate cortical regions.
Face-gender discrimination elicited activation in the lingual and fusiform gyri and in the Brodmann areas 22 and 38 in superior temporal sulcus (STS).
Biological motion recognition elicited activation in the lingual and fusiform gyri and in the Brodmann areas 22 and 38 in superior temporal sulcus (STS).
Biological motion recognition gave rise to strong responses in several known motion sensitive areas along the dorsal pathway.
Non-rigid direction discrimination gave rise to strong responses in several known motion sensitive areas along the dorsal pathway.
Recognition of biological motion stimuli may activate both the ventral and dorsal systems as well as their confluence in STS.
Stroke patients with unilateral brain lesions involving at least one of these areas correctly report the direction of the point-light walker but fail on the biological motion task.
Encoding of novel words in comparison with the first repetition was associated with a stronger activation in the left parahippocampal and inferior frontal gyrus.
Encoding of novel words compared to the second repetition was related to a greater bifrontal activation.
Recognition of studied items was associated with greater activation in the medial and bilateral inferior parietal lobe at first repetition in comparison with encoding of the novel items.
Recognition of studied items was associated with greater activation in the medial and left inferior parietal lobe at second repetition in comparison with encoding of the novel items.
Recognition at first repetition compared to recognition at second repetition was associated with greater bilateral frontal activation.
Significant clusters of activation were observed in left superior parietal lobe (SPL).
Significant clusters of activation were observed in the dorsal aspects of the inferior parietal cortex (IPC) bordering the SPL.
Localized clusters of activation were observed in the left premotor cortex.
Localized clusters of activation were observed in the sensorimotor cortex.
Localized clusters of activation were observed in the supplementary motor area.
No activation cluster was observed in the right hemisphere.
Within the parietal cortex, activation was significantly greater in the left SPL compared to left IPC.
Verbal working memory performance under non-interfering conditions activated Broca's area.
Verbal working memory performance under non-interfering conditions activated the left premotor cortex.
Verbal working memory performance under non-interfering conditions activated the cortex along the left intraparietal sulcus.
Verbal working memory performance under non-interfering conditions activated the right cerebellum.
No significant memory-related activation was found in these areas when silent articulatory suppression prevented the subjects from rehearsal.

Non-articulatory maintenance of phonological information was associated with enhanced activity in inferior parietal brain areas.
Phonological storage may be a function of a complex prefronto-parietal network.
Phonological storage is not localized in only one parietal brain region.
Analysis of the RT data confirmed the SI effect.

The left posterior superior temporal gyrus showed differential hemodynamic responses during the SI effect.
The left anterior cingulate cortex showed differential hemodynamic responses during the SI effect.
The bilateral orbitomedial prefrontal cortex showed differential hemodynamic responses during the SI effect.

Additional responses were observed in the left inferior parietal lobule.
Additional responses were observed in the right anterior temporal and occipital cortex.
The results are interpreted as indirectly supporting interactive models that allow spreading activation between both conceptual processing and phonological retrieval levels of word production.
The data confirm that selective attention/response suppression has a role in resolving the SI effect similar to the way in which Stroop interference is resolved.
Only patients with phantom limb pain showed a shift of the lip representation into the deafferented primary motor and somatosensory hand areas during lip movements.
Displacement of the lip representation in the primary motor and somatosensory cortex was positively correlated to the amount of phantom limb pain.
Thalamic activation was only present during executed movements in the healthy controls.
The cerebellum showed no evidence of reorganizational changes.
In amputees, movement of the intact hand showed a level of activation similar to movement of the right dominant hand in the healthy controls.

During imagination of moving the phantom hand, all patients showed significantly higher activation in the contralateral primary motor and somatosensory cortices compared with imagination of hand movements in the controls.
In the patients with phantom limb pain but not the pain-free amputees, imagined movement of the phantom hand activated the neighbouring face area.
These data suggest selective coactivation of the cortical hand and mouth areas in patients with phantom limb pain.
Recruitment of the motion-selective area MT-MST in hearing controls was greater when attention was directed centrally.

Deaf signers displayed enhanced recruitment of MT-MST during peripheral attention.

rTMS applied to the superior parietal lobule (SPL) causes a selective impairment of performance in comparison to a control group.
The parietal cortex is functionally important for the execution of spatial judgements on visually presented material.
TMS as an experimental tool has the potential to interfere with higher cognitive functions such as visuospatial information processing.

There were no significant differences between the primary somatosensory/primary motor/Brodmann area 40 (SI/MI/Ba40) and secondary somatosensory cortex (SII) activation volumes for normoxia and hyperoxia.

These results support a role of the amygdala in the appraisal of inherent value of choice.
These results support a role of the amygdala in the signaling of prospective negative outcomes.
Activation during the Stroop task replicated findings from two earlier fMRI studies.
These activations were remarkably similar to those observed during the Simon task.

These activations included anterior cingulate, supplementary motor, visual association, inferior temporal, inferior parietal, inferior frontal, and dorsolateral prefrontal cortices, as well as the caudate nuclei.

Resolution of interference effects in the Simon and Stroop tasks engage similar brain regions.
The neural systems that subserve successful task performance are likely to be similar.

These data underscore the importance of ventral premotor-cerebellar-parietal circuits in processing length and complexity of sequential finger movements.

Activation of the lateral geniculate nucleus was smaller with the eyes-open rest condition than with the eyes-closed rest condition during fixation or dim light room illumination.
Activations that can be attributed to ocular motor structures were larger with the eyes-closed rest condition than with the eyes-open rest condition.
BOLD signal decreases of cortical areas that represent visual, somatosensory, auditory, and vestibular functions were seen in the comparison fixation of light emitting diode (LED) minus eyes closed.

Activation of the right dorsal premotor area (PMd) extending to the posterior supplementary motor area (SMA) was significantly stronger during the parallel movements than during the mirror sequential movements.
Activation of the posterior SMA was significantly greater during the parallel movements than during the bimanual mirror movements or the unimanual movements of either hand.
Activation of the right PMd was significantly greater during the parallel movements than during the bimanual mirror movements or the unimanual movements of either hand.
The posterior SMA appears to be related to the bimanual coordination of finger movements.
The right PMd appears to be related to the bimanual coordination of finger movements.
Blood oxygen level-dependent (BOLD) fMRI signal in the amygdala was significantly higher during viewing of masked fearful faces than during the viewing of masked happy faces.
Significant signal increases in the amygdala occurred in response to masked fearful faces.
Significant signal decreases in the amygdala occurred in response to masked happy faces.
Masked fearful and happy facial expressions activated the sublenticular substantia innominata (SI).
Signal increases in the sublenticular substantia innominata were observed in response to both fearful and happy faces.

Brain areas in midlevels of the processing hierarchy, including extrastriate visual cortex extending into inferotemporal cortex and left dorsal prefrontal cortex, showed reductions in the amount of activation after repetition.
Early visual areas were activated equally by both novel and repeated objects and did not show effects of repetition.
Output motor areas were activated equally by both novel and repeated objects and did not show effects of repetition.
